title: Chapter 17: Through the Looking Glass
content: Morning light fell through Aline's windows like something owed — warm, ordinary, relentless. It pooled on the hardwood floor in golden rectangles and climbed the walls in slow, honest progression. The kind of light that didn't care about subterranean tunnels or server farms or men who hadn't felt the sun in two years.
Cass stood in the center of her living room like a man who'd landed on an alien planet.

His arms hung at his sides. His head tilted back slightly, eyes tracking the ceiling corners with the precision of a surveyor reading a blueprint. The smoke detector above the hallway. The pinhole behind the light switch plate. The fiber-optic thread he'd woven into the window casing. Every red dot he'd installed, every unblinking eye — they were all watching him now.
The irony sat on his chest like a stone.
"I know every camera angle," he murmured. His voice sounded wrong in this space. Too deep for her small apartment. Too real. "I never thought I'd be the one in the frame."

Aline leaned against the kitchen doorway, arms crossed, watching him catalog his own architecture. She'd imagined this moment a hundred ways — fury, betrayal, fear, revenge. She hadn't imagined the quiet devastation on his face. The way his fingers twitched at his sides like they wanted to reach up and dismantle everything he'd built.
"Come on," she said. "Let me show you around."

She led him through her apartment as if he hadn't already mapped every square inch from three states away. As if he hadn't memorized the creak in the third floorboard and the exact angle of light that hit her bed at 6:47 a.m. The tour was for him, yes — but it was also for her. She wanted to see his face at each stop. She wanted to watch him confront the evidence.

The kitchen first. Small, cluttered, smelling faintly of the lavender cleaner she favored. She opened the refrigerator. The organic greens, the free-range eggs, the oat milk — all replacements he'd orchestrated through grocery delivery services, swapping out her usual brands with surgical precision.
"You changed my entire diet," she said. Not a question.
"Atopic dermatitis risk reduction," he said automatically. Then, quieter: "You kept the eggs."
"They were good eggs."

He reached past her and touched the carton. Just touched it, his fingertips resting on the cardboard like it was evidence at a crime scene. His own crime. The pad of his index finger traced the label — pasture-raised, omega-3 enriched. The kind of detail he'd selected with the same cold calculation he used to route traffic through proxy servers.
She closed the refrigerator and moved to the nightstand. He followed, his footsteps careful and deliberate on her floor, a man navigating a minefield of his own making.

The nightstand held the vitamin bottles. B-complex. Vitamin D3 — 5,000 IU, because he'd calculated her deficiency from the pallor of her skin on camera. Iron, because her periods were heavy and he'd tracked the cycle. He picked up the D3 bottle, turned it in his hands. The plastic was half-empty.
"You kept taking them."
She shrugged. "They were good vitamins."
His mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. Something more raw than that — a fracture line running through the granite of his composure. He set the bottle down and his gaze drifted to the window.

The spot by the window. She'd stood there a hundred times — morning coffee, evening wine, watching the rain sheet down the glass. And every time, the smoke detector sixteen feet away had captured her in chiaroscuro, feeding her image through encrypted channels to a bunker beneath a building that didn't officially exist.
He walked to the window and looked up at the smoke detector. His jaw tightened.

"I watched you here," he said. No evasion. No apology. Just a fact, laid bare like a surgical incision. "You'd stand here and talk on the phone. Your mother, mostly. You'd pace. Three steps left, two steps right. You always touched the glass before you walked away."
Aline's throat tightened. She hadn't known he'd seen that. The glass-touching. The small, private ritual she'd never told anyone about.
"I did," she said.

He turned from the window, and the morning light caught his face properly for the first time. Pale. That was the first word — pale like something grown in darkness, which was exactly what he was. Hollows beneath his cheekbones. A faint tremor in his hands that he hid by putting them in his pockets. But his eyes were clear. Gray-green, watchful, absorbing her apartment like he was downloading it — except he already had. Months ago.
"Okay," she said, deliberately light. "Coffee."

The coffeemaker sat on her counter, a squat Mr. Coffee that had cost thirty-nine dollars and lasted four years through neglect and descaler runs she always forgot. She filled the carafe, dumped in grounds, pressed the button. The machine gurgled to life, and the smell hit within seconds — dark roast, slightly bitter, warm and familiar. The smell of mornings. Of normal life.
"You want to try?"

Cass approached the coffeemaker like it was a prototype he needed to reverse-engineer. He'd routed through smart home systems, hijacked IoT refrigerators to create botnets, exploited vulnerabilities in pacemakers. He'd brought a financial institution to its knees with a keyboard. He stared at the Mr. Coffee with the expression of a man facing a final boss.
"Put the carafe on the warming plate," Aline said. "Like that — no, the handle faces out. There."
He positioned the carafe. The coffee had finished brewing, and he reached for the carafe to pour, his hand closing around the glass directly above the warming plate.
A sharp hiss. He yanked his hand back.
"You have to use the handle," Aline said, fighting the smile that was threatening to break through her sternum. "The glass gets hot."

He stared at his reddened palm. This man who had infiltrated the power grid of a major American city. This man who had escaped a black-site server farm through a subway tunnel twelve hours ago. He had burned his hand on a Mr. Coffee because he didn't know how to use the handle.

The laugh escaped her before she could stop it. Not cruel — surprised. Genuine. The first real laugh she'd had in days, maybe weeks, and it broke out of her like something that had been clawing to get free.

Cass looked at her, and his expression cycled through confusion, mild offense, and finally — tentatively — something that might have been the beginning of a smile. It didn't quite land. His face seemed to have forgotten the mechanics.
"Here," she said, taking the carafe by the handle and pouring two mugs. She pressed one into his uninjured hand. "Drink. You'll feel more human."
He took a sip and made a sound — quiet, involuntary — that was close to pleasure. She wondered when he'd last had real coffee. Fresh coffee, not freeze-dried ration packets in a bunker.
"Thank you," he said. He meant the coffee. He meant more than the coffee.

They drank standing in her kitchen, shoulders almost touching, and for a moment the world outside — the Nest, VERA, the men who wanted Cass dead, the whole spinning machinery of threat and counter-threat — receded to a manageable distance. Just two people in a kitchen. Just morning light and the smell of coffee and the small, domestic strangeness of learning each other in three dimensions.
The knock at the door shattered it.
Aline glanced at Cass. His fingers tightened around his mug, and she watched his eyes catalog the room — exits, weapons, sight lines. Old habits, ingrained and instant. She touched his arm.
"It's Mags. I texted her."
His tension didn't ease. "You told her?"
"I tell Mags everything."
She opened the door, and Mags Delacroix swept in like a weather system — dark hair pulled back, winter coat still buttoned, eyes already scanning. Those eyes found Cass within two seconds.
Mags stopped.

She took him in — the pallor, the hollowed cheeks, the burned hand he was trying to hide against his thigh, the way he stood in Aline's kitchen like a man assembled from spare parts, some of them missing. Her expression cycled through the stages like a slot machine. Shock. Anger. Something that flickered and landed on a grudging, reluctant acceptance.
"So you're the phantom."

Cass straightened. For the first time in Aline's memory — and she had extensive memory of him, both as a voice and as a presence — he looked uncertain. Not calculating, not strategizing. Uncertain. The way a man looks when he's about to be judged by someone whose opinion matters.
"I prefer Cass."
Mags crossed her arms. "I prefer you stop spying on my best friend, but I guess we don't always get what we want."
Silence. Aline held her breath.

Mags studied him for a long, uncomfortable moment. She was looking for something — a flinch, a deflection, the tell of a man who didn't understand the magnitude of what he'd done. Whatever she found, it didn't send her running.
"He looks like crap," Mags said to Aline, jerking her chin toward Cass. "Has he eaten?"
"He burned his hand on the coffeemaker."
Mags's eyebrow arched. The ghost of amusement crossed her face, there and gone.
"He's a cyberterrorist who can't use a Mr. Coffee. That's almost poetic."
"I'm not a terrorist," Cass said quietly.

"Surveillance without consent. Manipulation of food and medication supply chains. Unauthorized access to—" He stopped, catching himself, and the corner of his mouth twisted. "The legal definition is complex."
"The moral one isn't," Mags said.
"No," he said. "It isn't."

They stared at each other. Aline watched the two people she trusted most — in radically different ways — measure each other across her kitchen counter. Mags, fierce and protective, standing for everything Aline should feel. Cass, pale and precise, offering nothing but the truth of what he'd done.
Mags broke first. She exhaled through her nose, a sharp, pragmatic sound.
"I'm going to go," she said. "I have class. But—" She pointed at Cass. "If you hurt her, I will find a way to hurt you back. And I'm creative."
"I don't doubt it," Cass said.

Mags looked at Aline. A conversation passed between them in the glance — seventeen years of friendship compressed into a single exchange. Then Mags was gone, the door clicking shut behind her, and the apartment settled back into stillness.
Cass exhaled. "She's terrifying."
"She's my best friend."
"Those aren't mutually exclusive."
Aline almost smiled again. Almost.

The afternoon passed in strange, quiet increments. Cass explored her apartment with the methodical attention of a man who knew the data but not the texture. He opened cabinets and closed them. He ran his finger along the spines of her books. He stood in the bathroom doorway for a long time, looking at the mirror, and she wondered if he was calculating the angle of the hidden camera or simply seeing himself — truly seeing himself — for the first time in years.

By evening, they found themselves on her bed. Sitting side by side, shoulders against the headboard, legs stretched out. The same bed where the red dot had blinked in rhythm with her heartbeat for months. The same mattress where she'd lain awake, talking to a voice in the dark, never knowing it was watching.
Cass looked up at the smoke detector. The tiny red light pulsed. On. Off. On.
"I can deactivate them," he said. "All of them. If you want."
The offer hung in the air. He could end it. One command and every camera in her apartment would go dark. The surveillance would stop. She'd be free of his gaze.
She considered. The power was hers, and she felt it — the unfamiliar weight of being the one who decided.
"Not yet," she said. "I want you to know what it feels like. Being watched. Every moment."

His face did something complicated. Guilt, first — the immediate, visceral kind that tightened the skin around his eyes. Then understanding, settling into the lines of his mouth like sediment. And beneath both, something that might have been respect. Recognition. A man confronting the mirror and accepting what he saw.
"Fair," he said.

Night came softly. The apartment darkened by degrees, streetlights painting orange bars across the ceiling, the sounds of the city filtering up through walls and windows — a car alarm, distant laughter, the hydraulic sigh of a bus kneeling at the corner.
Cass couldn't sleep.

He lay in her bed, rigid as a board, staring at the ceiling and feeling every camera in the room like a burn. The smoke detector. The light switch. The window casing. He knew the resolution of each feed. He knew that the smoke detector's night-vision mode switched on at 9:43 p.m., when the ambient light dropped below three lux. He knew that the infrared rendered the room in shades of gray, and that a body in the bed produced a heat signature like a slow, breathing comet.
His body. His heat signature. His exposure.

He'd built this system to be invisible. Now he was the one being rendered in infrared, and the vulnerability was a physical sensation — a pressure behind his sternum, a tightness in his throat, an acute awareness of every inch of his skin. He hadn't felt this exposed when he'd been running for his life through the tunnels beneath the city. This was different. This was being seen by choice. By her choice.

Beside him, Aline slept. Her breathing was slow and even, the rhythm of real sleep, and he found himself matching it — counting her exhales, syncing his own. The sheet had slipped down to her shoulder. Her hair fanned across the pillow in the half-dark. She smelled like her lavender cleaner and the coffee from this morning and something underneath that was just her, a scent he'd never captured on any feed.
He reached for her hand in the dark.
His fingers found hers — warm, slightly curled, the pads of her fingertips rougher than he'd imagined from watching. He half-expected her to pull away. To wake and question. To ask what he was doing.

She didn't. Her fingers uncurled and laced through his, a motion so natural it must have been unconscious. She held his hand in her sleep like it was the most obvious thing in the world. Like trust was a reflex, not a decision.
Cass stared at the ceiling. The red light on the smoke detector blinked — on, off, on — patient and impartial, watching them both.
This is what it feels like, he thought. Being observed. Being seen. Reduced to pixels and heat signatures by systems you built yourself.
And this is what it feels like to stay anyway.
He squeezed her hand, gently, and she made a small sound in her sleep and scooted closer. Her shoulder pressed against his arm. Her breath ghosted across his collarbone.

The phantom of the deep web, who had spent two years as a voice in the dark, an invisible architect of information and control, lay still in a woman's bed and let the cameras watch him choose to stay.
Terrifying. Exposed.
Worth it.
